items.mistake: Using 'AI' and 'machine learning' as exact synonyms
items.fix: Say AI for the field or product category; say ML when you mean learning from data. Reserve deep learning and generative AI for their sibling definitions.
items.why: Synonym collapse hides non-ML AI (search, rules) and makes architecture discussions imprecise.
items.example: A route optimizer using A* search is AI but not ML; a spam filter trained on labels is both AI and ML.

items.mistake: Calling any automation 'AI' because it uses heuristics
items.fix: Reserve AI for systems that perceive, reason, learn, or act toward goals requiring non-trivial intelligence—not every if–else branch.
items.why: Inflated labeling erodes trust and makes it impossible to discuss real capabilities or risks.
items.example: Sorting files by date is automation; classifying medical images from pixels is AI.

items.mistake: Assuming today's chatbots imply AGI is near
items.fix: Describe deployed systems as narrow AI with measurable task bounds; treat AGI as a separate research aspiration.
items.why: Capability overreach leads to misplaced safety assumptions and unrealistic product promises.
items.example: A model that writes fluent email can still fail arithmetic or cite nonexistent sources—narrow competence, not general intelligence.
